core_claim: Pasting production logs and code into unsanctioned chatbots moves secrets, customer data, and internal paths outside your controlled systems, and a clipboard paste slips past file-based data-loss tools.

slides.on_slide_copy: THE LOGS YOU PASTE INTO A CHATBOT LEAVE YOUR CONTROL FOR GOOD
slides.subline: Samsung learned it the hard way: engineers pasted source code into ChatGPT, and it banned the tools weeks later.

slides.on_slide_copy: Teams ban the tools instead of offering a safe one, so people just switch to personal accounts.

caption:
Pasting production logs into a chatbot feels like a shortcut. It is actually data leaving the systems you control, and you cannot pull it back.

This is not hypothetical. In 2023, Samsung engineers pasted internal source code and a recorded meeting into ChatGPT, and within weeks the company banned generative AI on its devices. Cyberhaven's analysis of around 1.6 million workers found that, as of mid-2023, 8.6 percent had pasted company data into ChatGPT and 4.7 percent had pasted confidential data. OWASP lists sensitive information disclosure as a top risk for LLM apps.

The reason it slips through is mechanical. A copy and paste strips the file and its classification, so file-based data-loss tools never see it move. Logs and code are the worst offenders, because they carry API tokens, customer IDs, stack traces, and internal endpoints.

The fix is not a ban. A ban with no safe path just pushes people to personal accounts, where you have even less visibility. Give people a sanctioned AI tool with redaction, logging, and a clear retention policy, plus one rule everyone understands: never paste production data.

Once it is pasted, you cannot get it back. Make the safe path easier than the risky one.

Does your team have a sanctioned way to use AI on real data, or just a rule nobody follows?

Follow for AI security breakdowns without the fake panic.
comment_prompt: Does your team have a sanctioned way to use AI on real data, or a rule everyone quietly ignores?
alt_text: Cover slide: a lone office photocopier in the dark mid-copy, a bright bar of light sweeping under the lid and a page easing out of the tray, with the headline in the lower third.
Context slide: a painted boundary line across a dark floor with loose papers drifting across it from one side to the other.
Risk slide: a tipped-over inkwell on a desk in the gloom, a thin bright rivulet of ink running off the edge.
Mechanism slide: scissors clipping a fragment out of a larger printed sheet, the cut piece lifting free and drifting away.
Failure point slide: a closed metal shutter pulled down over one doorway while a side door stands open beside it, papers slipping through the open one.
Defense slide: a document passing through a small inline filter cartridge, a cleaner redacted page coming out the other side.
Takeaway slide: a sheet of paper half dissolved into drifting particles in deep shadow, the intact half catching a thin rim of light.
CTA slide: a single rubber stamp resting beside an empty ink pad in vast dark negative space, with the comment question and handle.

sources.source: OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications (2025): LLM02 Sensitive Information Disclosure
sources.supports: Sensitive information disclosure (credentials, confidential business data, PII) is a top LLM-app risk; user-supplied input is a named vector.

sources.source: The Register: Samsung bans generative AI after staff leak
sources.supports: Samsung engineers pasted internal source code and a recorded meeting into ChatGPT (April 2023); Samsung banned generative AI on company devices.

sources.source: TechCrunch: Samsung bans ChatGPT after internal data leak
sources.supports: Independent confirmation of the Samsung source-code paste incident and the generative-AI ban.

sources.source: Cyberhaven: workers pasting company data into ChatGPT
sources.supports: As of June 2023, 8.6% of employees had pasted company data and 4.7% had pasted confidential data into ChatGPT; clipboard paste evades file-based DLP.

narration.text: The logs you paste into a chatbot leave your control, and you cannot pull them back.
Samsung learned this in 2023, when engineers pasted source code into ChatGPT, and the company banned the tools within weeks.
Copy and paste slips past file-based data-loss tools, so give people a sanctioned tool with redaction and logging, and one rule: never paste production data.
Once it is pasted, you cannot get it back. Make the safe path easier than the risky one.
Does your team have a sanctioned way to use AI on real data? Tell me below.
